chapters.title: Women began to speak up about their experiences, and this changed everything
chapters.text:
Many things look impossible until one brave person tries. Breaking the Harvey Weinstein story was one of them. Few people thought that the hidden acts of such a powerful Hollywood producer would ever be exposed to the world.

Jodi Kantor has written that harassers were often treated as cheeky bad boys, and even cheered on.

Most people brushed off his behavior as a rich man's habit and paid it no real attention, especially since he bragged about his feminist credentials in public. He gave loans to women's groups and joined marches against assault. Earlier investigations into his conduct found nothing solid, and many reporters tried without ever landing a story.

Everyone has the right to live and work without fear of being harmed by another person.

The courage that many victims of sexual abuse now show comes from the victory won by the women whose stories fill this summary. This piece shows that even the most powerful people who break the law can be held to account. It also gives hope in situations that feel hopeless. Read on to learn how eighty-seven women chose to break the silence around sexual harassment by sharing their stories.

chapters.title: It all began with Rose McGowan's decision to tell her story
chapters.text:
Rumors about Harvey Weinstein and his power over women had been swirling for years. At first, Rose McGowan refused to talk to a reporter after accusing him of rape. She did share social media posts that called out sexist movie roles and views. The claims were hard to swallow at first, since Weinstein had launched the careers of many young women, including Jennifer Lawrence, Michelle Williams, and Gwyneth Paltrow, along with male stars like Matt Damon. He had also helped with Hillary Clinton fundraisers, and Malia Obama had interned with him.

It is hard to believe abuse claims when the accused is a respected figure.

McGowan eventually told her full story. In 1997, she met Weinstein at the Sundance Film Festival to talk about roles, and he pushed her into a bathroom, stripped her, and forced his face between her legs. She got away by faking an orgasm. She received a hundred thousand dollar settlement, which she gave to a rape crisis center. The experience led her to see Hollywood as a system that used young women and threw them away.

Before the Times could publish, they had to give Weinstein a fair chance to respond, since there was no video and no witnesses. Rebecca Corbett, a long-time editor at the Times, helped Jodi Kantor see that more victims were out there. Jodi teamed up with Megan Twohey, who had covered Donald Trump's behavior toward women and had uncovered many cases of sexual crime. Megan had also seen how the police sometimes failed victims and how abusive doctors kept practicing. Jodi had started in 2013 looking into women's experiences at companies and other institutions, and she found many workers too afraid to speak up because they worried about losing their jobs.

The goal of the MeToo movement was to protect others from harm, not to rewrite the past.

One big reason victims came forward and joined the movement was that the reporters cared about shielding others from becoming victims.

chapters.title: There is strength in numbers: the more women that spoke up, the more evidence was made available
chapters.text:
Gathering testimony from leading ladies was hard. They guarded their public image and put walls around anyone trying to reach them.

Jodi combed through pictures from Cannes in France, looking for emails and phone numbers attached to women who had worked on Weinstein's films. When she did track someone down, the chats stayed short and went nowhere.

Fear of professional ruin kept many from cooperating with the investigation.

The bulk of Weinstein's ex-staff either clammed up or tossed out scraps that could not stand on their own. With a handful of helpers, Jodi managed to reach other actresses who, only after making her promise secrecy, opened up about an industry built on exploitation. Two of them, Daryl Hannah and Judith Godreche, said they had been hurt, but were too frightened to go further.

Gwyneth Paltrow met Weinstein at the Toronto Film Festival in 1994, when she was twenty-two. He dangled two projects in front of her, she signed on with Miramax, and her relationship with Brad Pitt began. True to form, Weinstein then summoned her to a hotel meeting and suggested they exchange massages. She made an excuse, told Brad, and he confronted Weinstein and warned him off. Weinstein bristled and threatened her, terrified she would bring up the hotel scene years later. Paltrow avoided a scandal and kept quiet until she sat down with Jodi. Once she realized a strong enough case could end Weinstein, she agreed to help connect Jodi to other survivors.

Paltrow decided to add her own account to the growing file of abuse stories.

Two additional women confirmed the same script, describing how Weinstein used business meetings as a cover for unwanted advances. Heavyweights like Angelina Jolie, Uma Thurman, and Salma Hayek each had their own tale of inappropriate conduct from him. Only Italian model Ambra Battilana Gutierrez had actually reported him to the police.

Plenty of abuse survivors keep silent rather than live with the stigma that often follows speaking up.

Once the first accounts landed, fresh voices kept arriving. People who had been inside Weinstein's circle for years began describing how the routine worked. Their stories lined up with the earlier testimony and gave lawyers and reporters a much firmer foundation to build on.

chapters.title: The more stories that were uncovered, the more courageous other victims became
chapters.text:
Megan kept digging through public records of Weinstein's behavior, which was hard because non-disclosure agreements destroyed the records and blocked anyone from sharing the names of complainants.

New harassment stories filled the news, and the pattern spread into other parts of the world. Men apologized, and victims were praised for their courage.

Ongoing exposure of sexual predators makes it safer for other victims to come forward.

Many former staff refused to testify, share what they knew, or even return calls. Amy Israel did speak up. She described how Weinstein had harassed her at the Toronto Film Festival after inviting her to a business meeting in his hotel. In 1998, at the Venice Film Festival, she noticed two of Weinstein's assistants, Zelda Perkins and Rowena Chiu, looking scared while Weinstein pretended nothing was wrong. Both had signed agreements to keep quiet after he had harassed them at different times while they worked for him.

Even with all of this, criminal proceedings could not move forward because there was not enough evidence.

Laura Madden then came forward. In 1992, Weinstein had masturbated in front of her under the cover of a business meeting. She still spent six years working for him afterward.

Even after the worst experiences, the women who worked for Weinstein wanted a chance to keep working, contributing, and succeeding.

chapters.title: Sexual predators like Harvey Weinstein always want to keep up appearances; your job is not to keep silent
chapters.text:
Jodi and Megan were warned not to be rude to other journalists, to watch for desperate moves from Weinstein, and to be careful never to speak off the record.

Weinstein had his lawyer, Lanny Davis, speak for him. Davis kept probing sources and claimed Weinstein was trying to fix the issues. After questions from Jodi and Megan, he admitted that a settlement had been made, then ran off saying he had to recheck his legal limits.

Weinstein tried to kill the investigation by hiring The Black Cube to remove evidence and stop women from testifying.

Nobody wanted the world to know they had risen in their career by helping a predator. Jodi found help in Irwin Reiter, Weinstein's top lieutenant and vice president of Accounting and Financial Reporting. They met for several nights at a spot Reiter chose, and as he shared what he knew, Jodi realized Weinstein had committed new offenses.

Wealthy and well-known abusers often pay their victims to stay silent.

Reiter described how Emily Nestor, Weinstein's temporary receptionist, was pushed until she left the office, and how Weinstein's personal assistant was forced to get an erectile dysfunction drug for him and was even given a bonus for it.

There was also an investigation into how six hundred thousand dollars raised at a 2015 AIDS charity auction had ended up in investors' accounts.

Bob, Weinstein's brother, was awkward and volatile, and he soon fought with Harvey over money. Bob said Harvey had lied to him about the settlements, and Bob saw the behavior as a form of excess. Soon after a physical fight with his brother, Bob went through a divorce and a drinking problem, and he left the business to get help.

The Weinstein Company had no rules that could hold Harvey accountable for his actions.

For context, a 2008 survey found that most victims had met the abuser willingly. Of the 129 victims identified, those aged seventeen and younger had met the abuser face to face in 74 percent of cases, and 93 percent of those meetings included sexual contact.

The team also heard from a former driver who described late-night pickups and the strange routes Weinstein asked for. Combined with the assistant's account of running odd errands, the picture of a man who treated women as tools became harder to dismiss.

chapters.title: Fear will keep you from speaking your truth, but once you do, you'll be free
chapters.text:
Lanny Davis, Weinstein's former lawyer, had told Megan roughly how many payments Weinstein had made. He thought it was between eight and twelve. That was not enough evidence. More proof was needed, and the fear of Weinstein's retaliation kept everyone worried about their reputation.

Jodi Kantor has said that there will never be a true end, and the point is for people to keep speaking up without fear.

At that point, it made sense to speak to Weinstein and his team and hear their side. With help from the Times' attorney and other editors, Jodi and Megan built a fireproof story.

Sexual offenders often blame the victim to excuse their acts.

Weinstein refused to back down. He ordered his IT staff to delete a document with women's contact information, and he pressured employees to sign a written statement saying they had enjoyed their time at the company.

Rowena Chiu had never told her husband. The confidentiality clauses had trapped all three of them in strange positions, including a woman who could not share her own experience with her spouse.

chapters.title: The threats from the opposition only made the reporters want to work even harder
chapters.text:
Word came back from the head of the Weinstein Company's California office. He was willing to share his side of the long-running story and to back up the dollar figure of the settlement. Weinstein's reply was sharp. He sent a message threatening to sue the Times. He tried to tear down the women's stories by lifting small photos and tidbits from their memoirs. He branded Laura Madden a liar. He demanded that Jodi and Megan hand over every document they had gathered.

The paper refused to back off. Reporters kept digging to put more victims on the record. In the end, Weinstein had to step away from the company and enter a treatment program at the board's request.

The story of Weinstein changed how the public expects men to treat women.

From October 2017 onward, the newsroom was flooded with tips. American women had been carrying a heavy load. Many posted MeToo stories online, sharing what men had done to them. The change was powered by accountability, by the knowledge that sharing actually led to action.

The Times' reporting team on sexual harassment grew and started covering restaurant staff, dancers, factory workers, and many other fields.

After the Weinstein case broke, the public took sexual crimes more seriously, largely because women chose to speak.

Louis C. K. lost distribution for his next film after five women shared detailed accounts of his actions. In the same window, Christine Blasey Ford, who had been assaulted in college by Brett Kavanaugh, now a Supreme Court associate justice, stepped forward after years of trying to bury what had happened to her.

Rumors of Donald Trump's paid-off relationship with a porn star also drew headlines. The flood of claims against powerful men left many readers asking tough questions about the law and about the truth of what these women had said.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
What you have just read is built from the real experiences of real people. Each woman who shared her story of being harassed by Trump, Weinstein, Kavanaugh, or anyone else in this summary did it so that you, the reader, would feel brave enough to do the same, or to stand behind others who do.

Weinstein leaned on his power and his long list of contacts to threaten everyone in the investigation, hoping they would back away. In the end, though, the head of his own company agreed to go on the record and speak up. Once that happened, more people stepped forward with their stories, and in some cases with proof, of what this serial abuser had done. The Weinstein case shows why so many molesters escape justice, especially when they have little reputation left to lose.

For a long time, the United States had a system that hushed claims of sexual harassment, and that system often shielded the harasser rather than stopping the harm. Women regularly signed documents giving up the right to discuss their own experiences. Abusers then moved on to new places and committed the same offenses all over again.

Encouraged by the other women who shared a common abuser, they each found the strength to speak. Every woman was a messenger from a different world, the battleground Midwest, the strange niche of show business, or the charged arena of the Supreme Court. Those differences did not split the group. They sparked curiosity and pulled the women closer together.

The Weinstein story moved other women to share similar experiences, and that wave of honesty changed how employers and ordinary people treated the people around them.

Try this. If you work in any organization, make sure it has clear, written rules against sexual harassment. Also, build a safe space at home where family and friends can speak out. If you lead a team, create a setting where staff can come forward with cases of abuse without fear of losing their job.
